summary: Gruber defends Apple's decision to prohibit third-party iPhone apps from running in the background, one week after the iPhone SDK launch. He explains that even Apple's own apps mostly follow this constraint, with only the four dock apps (Phone, Mail, Safari, iPod) running background processes. The restriction exists because the iPhone's 128 MB of RAM, limited CPU, and battery life cannot sustain multiple background processes. Gruber argues the trade-off benefits typical users by ensuring installed apps won't degrade phone performance or battery life. He notes the iPhone's UI simplicity partly derives from having no process management interface. He predicts the restriction will eventually be relaxed as hardware improves, but not soon.
key_points: Only four built-in dock apps run in the background; even Apple's other apps quit when not frontmost, maintaining a consistent policy
The iPhone's 128 MB RAM with no swap space makes background processes a genuine resource threat to performance and battery life
The absence of process management UI is a design feature, not a limitation — it eliminates 'computer administrative debris' from the user experience
Apple chose harsh restrictions first because it's far easier to relax constraints later than to impose them after the fact
The trade-off explicitly favors typical users over technically advanced users who might want background capabilities
money_quotes: There is no concept of launching or quitting — there is only switching.
The profound simplicity of the iPhone user interface stems in part from the complete lack of interface elements for managing processes.
If you want to know what's running, the answer is simply whatever app it is that you're looking at.
This is a trade-off, one in which technically advanced users pay the price for the benefit of typical users.
What's the alternative? A "don't install or launch too much crap" policy?
It is far easier to set harsh restrictions first and relax them later than the other way around.
In short, your iPhone will not be doing much while it's in your pocket.
key_insight: Apple's ban on background third-party apps is a deliberate design trade-off that preserves the iPhone's simplicity and reliability for typical users at the expense of power-user flexibility, and it's far easier to relax such restrictions later than to impose them after the fact.
